name: Forth Metacompilation
summary: A Forth system bootstrapped in two phases: a small seed metacompiler in a host language (C, Lisp) compiles a minimal Forth kernel; a Forth-written metacompiler then regenerates the full system. After phase 2, the host-language seed is no longer needed. lbForth (Lars Brinkhoff) is the canonical modern example.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Distinct from the bootstrapping coverage in `data/trusting-trust` is a pattern unique to the Forth tradition: **metacompilation**. A Forth system is bootstrapped in two phases. A "seed" metacompiler — typically written in a host language (C, Lisp) — first compiles a minimal Forth kernel with a handful of primitive CODE words. A *Forth-written* metacompiler then regenerates the full Forth system from source, including the metacompiler itself. After the second phase, the host-language seed is no longer needed.

sections.title: lbForth — the canonical example
sections.content:
**lbForth** (Lars Brinkhoff) is the canonical modern example. The bootstrap chain is:

1. **12 primitive C words** plus a small Lisp-written metacompiler (using SBCL, CLISP, CCL, or ECL) produce an initial Forth targeting C.
2. The resulting Forth runs a *Forth-written* metacompiler that regenerates the full system.
3. The same Forth metacompiler retargets to produce native kernels for Linux (x86), Windows, ARM, RISC-V, 68000, PDP-11, and asm.js, plus cross-compilers for 6502, 8051, AVR, Cortex-M, MSP430, PDP-8, PIC, and STM8.

All retargetability lives in a single architecture-specific file (`nucleus.fth`) plus an assembler written in Forth. Higher-level words live in portable `kernel.fth`. Adding a new architecture means writing one nucleus file and an assembler module — everything else is compiled from portable source.

sections.title: Lesson — retargetable compilers in tiny code budgets
sections.content:
Why this is distinct from the trust-chain bootstrapping in `data/trusting-trust`: that body of work focused on breaking the Thompson trust chain (`data/mrustc`, `data/gnu-mes-stage0`). Forth metacompilation is instead about **producing retargetable compilers in very small code budgets**. The same Forth kernel compiles to dozens of architectures from a handful of target-specific files, because the compiler itself is written in the language it compiles.

The transferable pattern for multi-architecture embedded deployment is to keep target-specific code isolated to a small plug-in module, write the compiler in the source language, and bootstrap once per new target rather than reimplementing the compiler.
